metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_1698
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Fig. 3C: synaptophysin staining is specifically reduced in neocortex and not in the hippocampus of ApoE KO and bEKO mice

Literal:
"Similar to previously published results, we observed a reduction in synaptophysin immunoreactivity in the neocortex of the ApoE KO mice (Masliah et al., 1995), and this was also observed in the bEKO mice (Fig. 3C). However, contrary to what was previously reported, we did not see a significant reduction in synaptophysin staining in the hippocampus in either the ApoE KO or bEKO mice (Fig. 3C)."

Fig 4. reduced theta-burst LTP and synaptic function in both ApoE KO and bEKO mice.

Literal:
"Slices from bEKO mice showed a reduction in LTP that was similar to ApoE KO slices (Fig. 4A,B). To further compare synaptic strength in these mice, input- output curves were calculated. Similar to the LTP findings, the bEKO and ApoE KO mice had reduced input- output responses compared with the ApoE3/E3
mice (Fig. 4C).". Experimental description: Experiments with a transgenic mouse expressing human APOE only in the peripheral tissue and crossed with an APOE KO mouse. This mouse, called bEKO, does not express APOE in the brain.

Literal M&M
"The bEKO mouse was generated as the result of the random integration of a human ApoE expression cassette containing an inadvertent mutation (R224Q; Sullivan et al., 1997). The site of transgenic integration has an expression profile that is restricted to peripheral tissues. To generate the bEKO mice, the transgenic mouse line was bred to homozygosity and then backcrossed to ApoE knock-outs (Zhang et al., 1992)."
